item.title: Ep 1 · Ch 1 — Emotion as the Family's Core Signaling System
item.description:
Chapter 1: Emotionally Focused Family Therapy
Establishes EFFT's founding premise: emotion is not a symptom but the family's rapid signaling system, so an eye roll or a raised voice is a bid for connection rather than a bad habit. Listeners learn to reflect the negative cycle, access the primary emotion beneath the reactive one, and stage an enactment instead of arguing content.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 1: Emotionally Focused Family Therapy
Establishes EFFT's founding premise: emotion is not a symptom but the family's rapid signaling system, so an eye roll or a raised voice is a bid for connection rather than a bad habit. Listeners learn to reflect the negative cycle, access the primary emotion beneath the reactive one, and stage an enactment instead of arguing content.

item.title: Ep 2 · Ch 1 — Choreographing Emotion Instead of Fixing the Behavior
item.description:
Chapter 1: Emotionally Focused Family Therapy
Synthesizes chapter one into a three-move strategy: track and externalize the negative cycle, reframe reactive behavior as attachment panic, and block the escape routes of blame and withdrawal by holding the present-moment experience. Includes the red flag that you have drifted off-model — the urge to hand out parenting advice or behavior charts.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 1: Emotionally Focused Family Therapy
Synthesizes chapter one into a three-move strategy: track and externalize the negative cycle, reframe reactive behavior as attachment panic, and block the escape routes of blame and withdrawal by holding the present-moment experience. Includes the red flag that you have drifted off-model — the urge to hand out parenting advice or behavior charts.

item.title: Ep 3 · Ch 1 — The Gentle Interrupt: Working a Room That Won't Stop Shouting
item.description:
Chapter 1: Emotionally Focused Family Therapy
A troubleshooting guide for families whose sessions dissolve into rapid-fire blame and escalation, built on the principle that noise is always a symptom of attachment distress and never the core problem. Listeners learn the gentle interruption, relentless validation of the pain beneath the volume, and why refereeing or taking sides only hands the cycle another win.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 1: Emotionally Focused Family Therapy
A troubleshooting guide for families whose sessions dissolve into rapid-fire blame and escalation, built on the principle that noise is always a symptom of attachment distress and never the core problem. Listeners learn the gentle interruption, relentless validation of the pain beneath the volume, and why refereeing or taking sides only hands the cycle another win.

item.title: Ep 4 · Ch 1 — When the Family Triggers the Therapist's Attachment System
item.description:
Chapter 1: Emotionally Focused Family Therapy
Turns the attachment lens on the clinician: how a dismissive parent or an out-of-control teen can pull the therapist into rescuing the identified patient or becoming the punitive one. Listeners learn to catch the reactive impulse, re-regulate, and treat countertransference as diagnostic data rather than acting it out.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 1: Emotionally Focused Family Therapy
Turns the attachment lens on the clinician: how a dismissive parent or an out-of-control teen can pull the therapist into rescuing the identified patient or becoming the punitive one. Listeners learn to catch the reactive impulse, re-regulate, and treat countertransference as diagnostic data rather than acting it out.

item.title: Ep 5 · Ch 2 — Reading a Child's Symptom as Family Distress Signal
item.description:
Chapter 2: Families and Emotion—Sharing the Language of Attachment
Learn how attachment theory and family systems theory converge to reframe one member's symptom as a co-constructed cycle rather than individual pathology. Covers externalizing the cycle, translating behavior into attachment meaning, and systemic reframes that make the pattern — not the person — the shared enemy.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 2: Families and Emotion—Sharing the Language of Attachment
Learn how attachment theory and family systems theory converge to reframe one member's symptom as a co-constructed cycle rather than individual pathology. Covers externalizing the cycle, translating behavior into attachment meaning, and systemic reframes that make the pattern — not the person — the shared enemy.

item.title: Ep 6 · Ch 2 — Translating Family Attacks into Cries for Help
item.description:
Chapter 2: Families and Emotion—Sharing the Language of Attachment
A synthesis of the moves that build a shared attachment language in the room: map the behavioral sequence, link each move to a primary emotion, then reframe the whole loop as mutual distress. Includes the red flag of drifting into behavior-chart territory and how to interrupt character labels with process labels.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 2: Families and Emotion—Sharing the Language of Attachment
A synthesis of the moves that build a shared attachment language in the room: map the behavioral sequence, link each move to a primary emotion, then reframe the whole loop as mutual distress. Includes the red flag of drifting into behavior-chart territory and how to interrupt character labels with process labels.

item.title: Ep 7 · Ch 2 — Parent Pursuit and Teen Withdrawal, Moment by Moment
item.description:
Chapter 2: Families and Emotion—Sharing the Language of Attachment
A session transcript is dissected to show adolescent rebellion as an autonomy bid laced with fear of parental rejection. Listeners see how the therapist catches the micro-sequence — mom leans in, teen looks away — slows it down, and elevates the teen's voice without dethroning the parent.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 2: Families and Emotion—Sharing the Language of Attachment
A session transcript is dissected to show adolescent rebellion as an autonomy bid laced with fear of parental rejection. Listeners see how the therapist catches the micro-sequence — mom leans in, teen looks away — slows it down, and elevates the teen's voice without dethroning the parent.

item.title: Ep 8 · Ch 2 — The No-Fault Clause: Blocking Blame at Source
item.description:
Chapter 2: Families and Emotion—Sharing the Language of Attachment
Blame is treated as secondary, reactive emotion protecting an injured self, not as a factual claim to be adjudicated. Listeners get concrete blame-blocking language, the markers that signal it ('you always...', 'on purpose'), and why letting blame sit unchallenged destroys emotional safety.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 2: Families and Emotion—Sharing the Language of Attachment
Blame is treated as secondary, reactive emotion protecting an injured self, not as a factual claim to be adjudicated. Listeners get concrete blame-blocking language, the markers that signal it ('you always...', 'on purpose'), and why letting blame sit unchallenged destroys emotional safety.

item.title: Ep 9 · Ch 2 — The Savior and the Probation Officer: Two Traps in Bonding With Everyone
item.description:
Chapter 2: Families and Emotion—Sharing the Language of Attachment
Explores the self-of-the-therapist demand of holding a secure bond with every family member at once while parents and children each lobby for a coalition. Listeners learn to decline the judge role, distribute empathy evenly, and catch the two roles the family keeps offering — rescuer of the child and enforcer for the parents.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 2: Families and Emotion—Sharing the Language of Attachment
Explores the self-of-the-therapist demand of holding a secure bond with every family member at once while parents and children each lobby for a coalition. Listeners learn to decline the judge role, distribute empathy evenly, and catch the two roles the family keeps offering — rescuer of the child and enforcer for the parents.

item.title: Ep 10 · Ch 3 — Why De-escalation Must Precede the Deep Work
item.description:
Chapter 3: Exploring the EFFT Process of Change
Maps the three-stage EFFT change process and explains why Stage 1 stabilization is a prerequisite for restructuring, not a preamble to it. Listeners learn how to block premature problem-solving, reflect the cycle in the room, and recognize the Stage 1 marker when a family names the pattern as its shared enemy.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 3: Exploring the EFFT Process of Change
Maps the three-stage EFFT change process and explains why Stage 1 stabilization is a prerequisite for restructuring, not a preamble to it. Listeners learn how to block premature problem-solving, reflect the cycle in the room, and recognize the Stage 1 marker when a family names the pattern as its shared enemy.

item.title: Ep 11 · Ch 3 — Pacing the Stages: Never Force Vulnerability Early
item.description:
Chapter 3: Exploring the EFFT Process of Change
A practical synthesis of how to pace interventions across de-escalation, restructuring, and consolidation. Listeners learn the rule of never asking for a primary emotion or attachment need while the cycle is still raging, and how to assess whether the room is cool enough to process vulnerability.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 3: Exploring the EFFT Process of Change
A practical synthesis of how to pace interventions across de-escalation, restructuring, and consolidation. Listeners learn the rule of never asking for a primary emotion or attachment need while the cycle is still raging, and how to assess whether the room is cool enough to process vulnerability.

item.title: Ep 12 · Ch 3 — The Therapist as Heat Shield in Reactive Families
item.description:
Chapter 3: Exploring the EFFT Process of Change
A case study of a family whose every conversation detonates, showing how attachment panic drives relentless reactivity. Listeners learn concrete down-regulation moves — the physical interrupt, the volume match, and the heat-shield reframe that turns an accusation into the terror underneath it.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 3: Exploring the EFFT Process of Change
A case study of a family whose every conversation detonates, showing how attachment panic drives relentless reactivity. Listeners learn concrete down-regulation moves — the physical interrupt, the volume match, and the heat-shield reframe that turns an accusation into the terror underneath it.

item.title: Ep 13 · Ch 3 — Failed Enactments and the Retreat to Safety
item.description:
Chapter 3: Exploring the EFFT Process of Change
Troubleshoots premature restructuring: what happens when a child is asked to share a need with a parent still too dysregulated to receive it. Listeners learn how to test readiness before an enactment, spot the parent's tension or quick justification, and pull back to de-escalation instead of pushing the child to try again.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 3: Exploring the EFFT Process of Change
Troubleshoots premature restructuring: what happens when a child is asked to share a need with a parent still too dysregulated to receive it. Listeners learn how to test readiness before an enactment, spot the parent's tension or quick justification, and pull back to de-escalation instead of pushing the child to try again.

item.title: Ep 14 · Ch 3 — Therapist Anxiety and the Urge to Rush Families
item.description:
Chapter 3: Exploring the EFFT Process of Change
Examines the self-of-the-therapist during slow de-escalation, and how a clinician's own need for competence and visible results pushes them toward teaching and solutions instead of experiential work. Listeners learn internal checks for distinguishing interventions made for the family's benefit from those made to relieve their own performance anxiety.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 3: Exploring the EFFT Process of Change
Examines the self-of-the-therapist during slow de-escalation, and how a clinician's own need for competence and visible results pushes them toward teaching and solutions instead of experiential work. Listeners learn internal checks for distinguishing interventions made for the family's benefit from those made to relieve their own performance anxiety.

item.title: Ep 15 · Ch 4 — Tracking the Cycle While Building the Family Alliance
item.description:
Chapter 4: Alliance and Assessment of Family Patterns
Steps 1 and 2 of EFFT: how the therapist builds a multi-directional alliance that is a safe haven for the parent's failure and the child's rejection at the same time, while assessment happens by watching the negative pattern enact itself in the room. Listeners learn to stay with process over content and to build a cycle map the family will actually agree with.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 4: Alliance and Assessment of Family Patterns
Steps 1 and 2 of EFFT: how the therapist builds a multi-directional alliance that is a safe haven for the parent's failure and the child's rejection at the same time, while assessment happens by watching the negative pattern enact itself in the room. Listeners learn to stay with process over content and to build a cycle map the family will actually agree with.

item.title: Ep 16 · Ch 4 — Multi-Directed Partiality: Balancing Validation Across the Family
item.description:
Chapter 4: Alliance and Assessment of Family Patterns
A practical playbook for relentless, balanced validation — every time you validate a parent's exhaustion you must turn and validate how heavy that is for the child to hear. Includes the red-flag marker (a member going silent or crossing their arms after you've spent time with someone else) and language to rehearse before the next session.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 4: Alliance and Assessment of Family Patterns
A practical playbook for relentless, balanced validation — every time you validate a parent's exhaustion you must turn and validate how heavy that is for the child to hear. Includes the red-flag marker (a member going silent or crossing their arms after you've spent time with someone else) and language to rehearse before the next session.

item.title: Ep 17 · Ch 4 — Declining the Family's Invitation to Take Sides
item.description:
Chapter 4: Alliance and Assessment of Family Patterns
How therapists get pulled into coalitions — siding with the reasonable parent against the unruly teen — and how to detect and dismantle the invitation to collude when a family looks to you to judge. The clinical move is to name the invitation and reframe it systemically, proving safety through understanding the cycle rather than through verdicts.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 4: Alliance and Assessment of Family Patterns
How therapists get pulled into coalitions — siding with the reasonable parent against the unruly teen — and how to detect and dismantle the invitation to collude when a family looks to you to judge. The clinical move is to name the invitation and reframe it systemically, proving safety through understanding the cycle rather than through verdicts.

item.title: Ep 18 · Ch 4 — Metabolizing Moral Judgment Into Clinical Curiosity
item.description:
Chapter 4: Alliance and Assessment of Family Patterns
Neutrality in EFFT is not detachment but equal emotional investment in everyone's pain, and it breaks down when a rigid father or a contemptuous teen triggers the therapist's own values. Listeners learn to catch that internal reaction before it leaks through tone or body language, and to find the protective intent and attachment panic driving the behavior.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 4: Alliance and Assessment of Family Patterns
Neutrality in EFFT is not detachment but equal emotional investment in everyone's pain, and it breaks down when a rigid father or a contemptuous teen triggers the therapist's own values. Listeners learn to catch that internal reaction before it leaks through tone or body language, and to find the protective intent and attachment panic driving the behavior.

item.title: Ep 19 · Ch 5 — Honoring the Wall Behind "I Don't Care"
item.description:
Chapter 5: Working Through Relationship Blocks
How a relational block works in Steps 3 and 4 of EFFT: secondary reactivity (shrugs, numbness, "I don't care") shields the primary attachment need underneath. Listeners learn to validate the block's protective function rather than bulldoze it, and to reframe the problem as caring having become too dangerous.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 5: Working Through Relationship Blocks
How a relational block works in Steps 3 and 4 of EFFT: secondary reactivity (shrugs, numbness, "I don't care") shields the primary attachment need underneath. Listeners learn to validate the block's protective function rather than bulldoze it, and to reframe the problem as caring having become too dangerous.

item.title: Ep 20 · Ch 5 — Evocative Responding and the RISSSC Slowdown
item.description:
Chapter 5: Working Through Relationship Blocks
A practical playbook for moving a family from analytical talk about emotion to felt emotion in the room, using RISSSC (repeat, image, simple, slow, soft, client's words). Includes the red flag of answering an intellectualized statement with a cognitive question, and how to seize a client's metaphor instead.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 5: Working Through Relationship Blocks
A practical playbook for moving a family from analytical talk about emotion to felt emotion in the room, using RISSSC (repeat, image, simple, slow, soft, client's words). Includes the red flag of answering an intellectualized statement with a cognitive question, and how to seize a client's metaphor instead.

item.title: Ep 21 · Ch 5 — The Critical Father's Panic Beneath the Lecture
item.description:
Chapter 5: Working Through Relationship Blocks
A case study of a harshly critical father and his withdrawn son, where the father's anger is disproportionate to the grades but proportional to his terror of failing as a parent. Listeners learn to bypass the secondary anger with empathic conjecture and speak directly to the panicked, loving father beneath the criticism.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 5: Working Through Relationship Blocks
A case study of a harshly critical father and his withdrawn son, where the father's anger is disproportionate to the grades but proportional to his terror of failing as a parent. Listeners learn to bypass the secondary anger with empathic conjecture and speak directly to the panicked, loving father beneath the criticism.

item.title: Ep 22 · Ch 5 — Treating Secondary Emotions Like Guard Dogs
item.description:
Chapter 5: Working Through Relationship Blocks
What to do when sessions get stuck cycling in anger, blame, and numbness instead of moving toward vulnerability. Listeners learn to soothe the "guard dog" rather than argue with the content of the anger, and to spot the cycling marker that signals it is time to interrupt.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 5: Working Through Relationship Blocks
What to do when sessions get stuck cycling in anger, blame, and numbness instead of moving toward vulnerability. Listeners learn to soothe the "guard dog" rather than argue with the content of the anger, and to spot the cycling marker that signals it is time to interrupt.

item.title: Ep 23 · Ch 5 — Regulating Your Own Anxiety When Clients Stonewall
item.description:
Chapter 5: Working Through Relationship Blocks
A self-of-the-therapist episode on the frustration and sense of failure that arise when a client answers "I don't know" for the fifth time. Listeners learn to shift the goal from breaking the block to understanding its purpose, and to check whether they are pushing for the client's readiness or their own reassurance.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 5: Working Through Relationship Blocks
A self-of-the-therapist episode on the frustration and sense of failure that arise when a client answers "I don't know" for the fifth time. Listeners learn to shift the goal from breaking the block to understanding its purpose, and to check whether they are pushing for the client's readiness or their own reassurance.

item.title: Ep 24 · Ch 6 — Choreographing the Enactment of Disowned Attachment Needs
item.description:
Chapter 6: Engaging Family Vulnerability
Stage 2 (Steps 5 and 6) asks the child to access and voice a disowned attachment need while the parent is coached to receive it. Learn how the therapist reflects the primary fear, sets up the enactment, and processes the parent's response in the moment.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 6: Engaging Family Vulnerability
Stage 2 (Steps 5 and 6) asks the child to access and voice a disowned attachment need while the parent is coached to receive it. Learn how the therapist reflects the primary fear, sets up the enactment, and processes the parent's response in the moment.

item.title: Ep 25 · Ch 6 — Prepping the Parent Before the Child Risks
item.description:
Chapter 6: Engaging Family Vulnerability
A three-phase strategy for a corrective emotional experience: prep the receiving parent to listen without fixing, help the child deliver one distilled primary-emotion message, then process the exchange with both immediately. The red flag is vulnerability left hanging unanswered, which the child reads as rejection.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 6: Engaging Family Vulnerability
A three-phase strategy for a corrective emotional experience: prep the receiving parent to listen without fixing, help the child deliver one distilled primary-emotion message, then process the exchange with both immediately. The red flag is vulnerability left hanging unanswered, which the child reads as rejection.

item.title: Ep 26 · Ch 6 — Slicing It Thin With an Angry Teenager
item.description:
Chapter 6: Engaging Family Vulnerability
A session transcript where a hostile teen's anger masks a fear of abandonment, and the therapist asks for one sentence rather than a speech. You'll see how physical anchoring and the empathic hold carry a client through the terrifying moment of saying 'I'm scared' out loud.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 6: Engaging Family Vulnerability
A session transcript where a hostile teen's anger masks a fear of abandonment, and the therapist asks for one sentence rather than a speech. You'll see how physical anchoring and the empathic hold carry a client through the terrifying moment of saying 'I'm scared' out loud.

item.title: Ep 27 · Ch 6 — Catching the Defensive But Before It Wounds
item.description:
Chapter 6: Engaging Family Vulnerability
When a parent answers a child's fear with justification or minimization, the risk is a fresh attachment injury. Learn the 'catch and reframe' move: block the defense, validate the parent's underlying panic about failing, and redirect them to the child's emotion.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 6: Engaging Family Vulnerability
When a parent answers a child's fear with justification or minimization, the risk is a fresh attachment injury. Learn the 'catch and reframe' move: block the defense, validate the parent's underlying panic about failing, and redirect them to the child's emotion.

item.title: Ep 28 · Ch 6 — Sitting on Your Hands When the Child Cries
item.description:
Chapter 6: Engaging Family Vulnerability
Therapist overprotectiveness turns the clinician into a surrogate secure base and quietly disempowers the parent. This episode covers recognizing the rescue urge, tolerating the silence, and redirecting the child's need for comfort back to the caregiver where it belongs.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 6: Engaging Family Vulnerability
Therapist overprotectiveness turns the clinician into a surrogate secure base and quietly disempowers the parent. This episode covers recognizing the rescue urge, tolerating the silence, and redirecting the child's need for comfort back to the caregiver where it belongs.

item.title: Ep 29 · Ch 7 — Shaping the Parent's Response Past Reassuring Logic
item.description:
Chapter 7: Restructuring Attachment and Caregiving Responses
Step 7 hinges on the caregiving response: when a child says "I'm terrified I'm not good enough," parents reach for evidence and logic, and the therapist must redirect them into an accessible, responsive, engaged emotional message. Listeners learn how to coach a parent out of reassurance and into an enactment that speaks directly to the child's attachment fear.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 7: Restructuring Attachment and Caregiving Responses
Step 7 hinges on the caregiving response: when a child says "I'm terrified I'm not good enough," parents reach for evidence and logic, and the therapist must redirect them into an accessible, responsive, engaged emotional message. Listeners learn how to coach a parent out of reassurance and into an enactment that speaks directly to the child's attachment fear.

item.title: Ep 30 · Ch 7 — Did It Land? The Post-Enactment Reception Check
item.description:
Chapter 7: Restructuring Attachment and Caregiving Responses
A caregiving message is not delivered until the child opens it, so the therapist turns immediately to the child after the enactment to process reception rather than assuming the right words finished the job. Listeners get the concrete reception-check prompts and the markers of deflection that signal a block to receiving care.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 7: Restructuring Attachment and Caregiving Responses
A caregiving message is not delivered until the child opens it, so the therapist turns immediately to the child after the enactment to process reception rather than assuming the right words finished the job. Listeners get the concrete reception-check prompts and the markers of deflection that signal a block to receiving care.

item.title: Ep 31 · Ch 7 — When a Child Rejects Care, Keep the Parent Present
item.description:
Chapter 7: Restructuring Attachment and Caregiving Responses
"You don't mean that" is self-protection after an attachment injury, not a lack of love, and the danger is the wounded parent withdrawing in response. Listeners learn to validate the child's skepticism while coaching the parent to stay engaged and offer the message again.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 7: Restructuring Attachment and Caregiving Responses
"You don't mean that" is self-protection after an attachment injury, not a lack of love, and the danger is the wounded parent withdrawing in response. Listeners learn to validate the child's skepticism while coaching the parent to stay engaged and offer the message again.

item.title: Ep 32 · Ch 7 — Becoming a Reverent Witness to the Family's Repair
item.description:
Chapter 7: Restructuring Attachment and Caregiving Responses
In the moment of profound connection the therapist's role shifts from orchestrator to witness, and the trap is over-talking to manage one's own surge of joy and discomfort with positive emotion. Listeners learn to hold silence, offer a minimal anchor, and let the family own the success.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 7: Restructuring Attachment and Caregiving Responses
In the moment of profound connection the therapist's role shifts from orchestrator to witness, and the trap is over-talking to manage one's own surge of joy and discomfort with positive emotion. Listeners learn to hold silence, offer a minimal anchor, and let the family own the success.

item.title: Ep 33 · Ch 8 — Facing Old Problems From New Secure Positions
item.description:
Chapter 8: Consolidating Security in the Family System
Stage 3 (Steps 8 and 9) turns the family's new attachment security toward the pragmatic conflicts that used to detonate them, and the therapist steps back from orchestrating to highlighting. Listeners learn how to spotlight self-correction and repair in the room and help the family author a new story of who they are.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 8: Consolidating Security in the Family System
Stage 3 (Steps 8 and 9) turns the family's new attachment security toward the pragmatic conflicts that used to detonate them, and the therapist steps back from orchestrating to highlighting. Listeners learn how to spotlight self-correction and repair in the room and help the family author a new story of who they are.

item.title: Ep 34 · Ch 8 — Letting the Family Fail Softly and Self-Repair
item.description:
Chapter 8: Consolidating Security in the Family System
A hands-on playbook for the consolidation phase: introduce a mild stressor, hold your tongue for ten seconds, and let the family slip toward the old cycle so they can catch and repair it themselves. The episode covers the red flag of rescuing too fast, the marker of spontaneous repair, and the self-check on whether they still need you.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 8: Consolidating Security in the Family System
A hands-on playbook for the consolidation phase: introduce a mild stressor, hold your tongue for ten seconds, and let the family slip toward the old cycle so they can catch and repair it themselves. The episode covers the red flag of rescuing too fast, the marker of spontaneous repair, and the self-check on whether they still need you.

item.title: Ep 35 · Ch 8 — A Slip Is Not a Fall: Dissecting Relapse
item.description:
Chapter 8: Consolidating Security in the Family System
How to troubleshoot the inevitable return of the negative cycle without letting it destroy the family's new confidence. Listeners learn to lean in with curiosity rather than disappointment, walk the family through the exact moment attachment panic was triggered, and challenge all-or-nothing talk like "we're right back where we started."
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 8: Consolidating Security in the Family System
How to troubleshoot the inevitable return of the negative cycle without letting it destroy the family's new confidence. Listeners learn to lean in with curiosity rather than disappointment, walk the family through the exact moment attachment panic was triggered, and challenge all-or-nothing talk like "we're right back where we started."

item.title: Ep 36 · Ch 8 — Termination Panic and the Therapist's Own Attachment
item.description:
Chapter 8: Consolidating Security in the Family System
Families often manufacture a crisis as therapy ends—separation anxiety dressed up as regression—and the therapist must resist dropping back into Stage 1 crisis mode. This episode examines the self-of-the-therapist work of naming the termination fear, avoiding fostered dependency, and handing the reins back with confidence.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 8: Consolidating Security in the Family System
Families often manufacture a crisis as therapy ends—separation anxiety dressed up as regression—and the therapist must resist dropping back into Stage 1 crisis mode. This episode examines the self-of-the-therapist work of naming the termination fear, avoiding fostered dependency, and handing the reins back with confidence.

item.title: Ep 37 · Ch 9 — Depression as a Wall Against Parental Comfort
item.description:
Chapter 9: Case Example—EFFT and Treating an Internalizing Disorder
Reframes childhood anxiety and depression as attachment loneliness, showing how the symptom sets up a cycle of mutual helplessness in which terrified parents hover or withdraw and the child pulls further away. Listeners learn to pivot the family from curing the illness to reaching the isolated child through enactments and externalizing the symptom.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 9: Case Example—EFFT and Treating an Internalizing Disorder
Reframes childhood anxiety and depression as attachment loneliness, showing how the symptom sets up a cycle of mutual helplessness in which terrified parents hover or withdraw and the child pulls further away. Listeners learn to pivot the family from curing the illness to reaching the isolated child through enactments and externalizing the symptom.

item.title: Ep 38 · Ch 9 — Coaching Parents to Sit in the Dark
item.description:
Chapter 9: Case Example—EFFT and Treating an Internalizing Disorder
A practical playbook for blocking the parent's instinct to fix, minimize, or silver-line a child's distress and coaching co-regulating presence instead. Includes the in-session interruption when a parent jumps to breathing techniques, and the markers that signal it's time to validate pain rather than solve it.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 9: Case Example—EFFT and Treating an Internalizing Disorder
A practical playbook for blocking the parent's instinct to fix, minimize, or silver-line a child's distress and coaching co-regulating presence instead. Includes the in-session interruption when a parent jumps to breathing techniques, and the markers that signal it's time to validate pain rather than solve it.

item.title: Ep 39 · Ch 9 — School Refusal and the Shame Beneath Anxiety
item.description:
Chapter 9: Case Example—EFFT and Treating an Internalizing Disorder
A session-transcript case study of a paralyzed, school-refusing child whose panic is really a fear of losing parental approval. Listeners learn to translate the parents' exasperated sighs into the rejection the child hears, and to shift the goal from attendance to healing shame.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 9: Case Example—EFFT and Treating an Internalizing Disorder
A session-transcript case study of a paralyzed, school-refusing child whose panic is really a fear of losing parental approval. Listeners learn to translate the parents' exasperated sighs into the rejection the child hears, and to shift the goal from attendance to healing shame.

item.title: Ep 40 · Ch 9 — Radical Validation Instead of the Fixing Reflex
item.description:
Chapter 9: Case Example—EFFT and Treating an Internalizing Disorder
Troubleshoots the therapist's urge to make a depressed child's pain disappear, including how to stay present when a child says "I wish I were dead" rather than flinching into platitudes or premature safety talk. Covers the red-flag reassurance responses and how to model tolerating despair for panicked parents.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 9: Case Example—EFFT and Treating an Internalizing Disorder
Troubleshoots the therapist's urge to make a depressed child's pain disappear, including how to stay present when a child says "I wish I were dead" rather than flinching into platitudes or premature safety talk. Covers the red-flag reassurance responses and how to model tolerating despair for panicked parents.

item.title: Ep 41 · Ch 9 — When the Therapist Absorbs the Family's Hopelessness
item.description:
Chapter 9: Case Example—EFFT and Treating an Internalizing Disorder
A self-of-the-therapist episode on catching a family's despair, losing clinical edge, and secretly believing the family is beyond repair. Listeners learn to name the absorbed hopelessness in-session and convert it into an empathic bridge to the family's daily reality.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 9: Case Example—EFFT and Treating an Internalizing Disorder
A self-of-the-therapist episode on catching a family's despair, losing clinical edge, and secretly believing the family is beyond repair. Listeners learn to name the absorbed hopelessness in-session and convert it into an empathic bridge to the family's daily reality.

item.title: Ep 42 · Ch 10 — Anger as Armor: Defiance Read as Attachment Protest
item.description:
Chapter 10: Case Example—EFFT and Treating an Externalizing Disorder
Reframes aggression, rule-breaking, and defiance as a child's protest against lost connection rather than a character flaw, and traces the cycle of escalating mutual threat between crackdown and rebellion. Listeners get the therapist moves that speak past the provocation to the fear underneath it.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 10: Case Example—EFFT and Treating an Externalizing Disorder
Reframes aggression, rule-breaking, and defiance as a child's protest against lost connection rather than a character flaw, and traces the cycle of escalating mutual threat between crackdown and rebellion. Listeners get the therapist moves that speak past the provocation to the fear underneath it.

item.title: Ep 43 · Ch 10 — Soothing the Wardens: Softening Punitive Parental Anger
item.description:
Chapter 10: Case Example—EFFT and Treating an Externalizing Disorder
A practical strategy for validating exhausted parents while blocking their punitive attacks, so they can grieve the 'ideal' child and shift from behavior modification to relational repair. Includes the discipline bypass and the marker that a parent citing consequences is signaling attachment panic.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 10: Case Example—EFFT and Treating an Externalizing Disorder
A practical strategy for validating exhausted parents while blocking their punitive attacks, so they can grieve the 'ideal' child and shift from behavior modification to relational repair. Includes the discipline bypass and the marker that a parent citing consequences is signaling attachment panic.

item.title: Ep 44 · Ch 10 — If I Show You My Worst: Sabotage as Test
item.description:
Chapter 10: Case Example—EFFT and Treating an Externalizing Disorder
A session breakdown of an adolescent who insults everyone in the room to confirm his fear that he is unlovable, and how the parents' embarrassed withdrawal proves him right. Shows how naming the test and staying steadfastly engaged interrupts the cycle of rejection.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 10: Case Example—EFFT and Treating an Externalizing Disorder
A session breakdown of an adolescent who insults everyone in the room to confirm his fear that he is unlovable, and how the parents' embarrassed withdrawal proves him right. Shows how naming the test and staying steadfastly engaged interrupts the cycle of rejection.

item.title: Ep 45 · Ch 10 — Dropping the Rope When a Client Refuses
item.description:
Chapter 10: Case Example—EFFT and Treating an Externalizing Disorder
Why any power struggle a therapist enters with a defiant client is already lost, and how radical surrender of control turns refusal into material to explore. Teaches treating 'I don't want to' as a protective boundary rather than stubbornness, and checking whether resistance triggers your own need for control.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 10: Case Example—EFFT and Treating an Externalizing Disorder
Why any power struggle a therapist enters with a defiant client is already lost, and how radical surrender of control turns refusal into material to explore. Teaches treating 'I don't want to' as a protective boundary rather than stubbornness, and checking whether resistance triggers your own need for control.

item.title: Ep 46 · Ch 10 — Staying Grounded When a Client's Rage Fills the Room
item.description:
Chapter 10: Case Example—EFFT and Treating an Externalizing Disorder
Addresses the therapist's own fight/flight/freeze response to aggressive, screaming, or physically imposing clients, and how fear quietly bends clinical decisions toward appeasement or authoritarian rigidity. Listeners learn grounding and calm-assertive moves that give the client a corrective experience: their anger cannot drive you away.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 10: Case Example—EFFT and Treating an Externalizing Disorder
Addresses the therapist's own fight/flight/freeze response to aggressive, screaming, or physically imposing clients, and how fear quietly bends clinical decisions toward appeasement or authoritarian rigidity. Listeners learn grounding and calm-assertive moves that give the client a corrective experience: their anger cannot drive you away.

item.title: Ep 47 · Ch 11 — Stepfamilies Are Born From Loss, Not Blending
item.description:
Chapter 11: Case Example—EFFT and Working with Stepfamilies: "We're Not Blended, We're Lumpy"
Maps the stepfamily cycle of inclusion and exclusion, where a stepparent's effort to connect reads as invasion and the biological parent gets stuck refereeing. Shows why securing the biological parent-child bond first, rather than handing the stepparent disciplinary authority, is what eventually makes room for the stepparent.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 11: Case Example—EFFT and Working with Stepfamilies: "We're Not Blended, We're Lumpy"
Maps the stepfamily cycle of inclusion and exclusion, where a stepparent's effort to connect reads as invasion and the biological parent gets stuck refereeing. Shows why securing the biological parent-child bond first, rather than handing the stepparent disciplinary authority, is what eventually makes room for the stepparent.

item.title: Ep 48 · Ch 11 — Why the Stepparent Must Step Back From Discipline
item.description:
Chapter 11: Case Example—EFFT and Working with Stepfamilies: "We're Not Blended, We're Lumpy"
A practical strategy for taking the pressure to blend off a family: validate the grief for the original family, normalize the awkwardness, and explicitly assign discipline to the biological parent. Flags the red flag of coaching a stepparent on how to win the child over.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 11: Case Example—EFFT and Working with Stepfamilies: "We're Not Blended, We're Lumpy"
A practical strategy for taking the pressure to blend off a family: validate the grief for the original family, normalize the awkwardness, and explicitly assign discipline to the biological parent. Flags the red flag of coaching a stepparent on how to win the child over.

item.title: Ep 49 · Ch 11 — Becky's Loyalty Test and the Frozen Mother
item.description:
Chapter 11: Case Example—EFFT and Working with Stepfamilies: "We're Not Blended, We're Lumpy"
Works the Sharon-Becky-Frank triangle, where a displaced teenager attacks her stepfather to test whether her mother will still choose her. Demonstrates the enactment in which mom reassures her daughter that her place is irreplaceable, and how that reassurance lowers hostility toward the stepparent.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 11: Case Example—EFFT and Working with Stepfamilies: "We're Not Blended, We're Lumpy"
Works the Sharon-Becky-Frank triangle, where a displaced teenager attacks her stepfather to test whether her mother will still choose her. Demonstrates the enactment in which mom reassures her daughter that her place is irreplaceable, and how that reassurance lowers hostility toward the stepparent.

item.title: Ep 50 · Ch 11 — Mandatory Movie Night: When Forced Togetherness Backfires
item.description:
Chapter 11: Case Example—EFFT and Working with Stepfamilies: "We're Not Blended, We're Lumpy"
Explains why parental demands for closeness, from enforced family dinners to "we are a family now," invalidate children's grief and harden their resistance. Listeners learn language for protecting a child's right to distance while helping parents sit with their own disappointment.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 11: Case Example—EFFT and Working with Stepfamilies: "We're Not Blended, We're Lumpy"
Explains why parental demands for closeness, from enforced family dinners to "we are a family now," invalidate children's grief and harden their resistance. Listeners learn language for protecting a child's right to distance while helping parents sit with their own disappointment.

item.title: Ep 51 · Ch 12 — Frozen Grief and the Family's Mutual Protective Silence
item.description:
Chapter 12: Case Example—EFFT and Traumatic Loss
How traumatic loss such as a sibling's suicide shatters a family's assumption of safety and produces "frozen grief," where members withdraw to avoid burdening each other and end up emotionally deprived exactly when they need connection. Listeners learn therapist language for naming the unspeakable and connecting isolated grievers rather than comforting one member while the rest watch.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 12: Case Example—EFFT and Traumatic Loss
How traumatic loss such as a sibling's suicide shatters a family's assumption of safety and produces "frozen grief," where members withdraw to avoid burdening each other and end up emotionally deprived exactly when they need connection. Listeners learn therapist language for naming the unspeakable and connecting isolated grievers rather than comforting one member while the rest watch.

item.title: Ep 52 · Ch 12 — Titrating Trauma Narrative into Shared Family Mourning
item.description:
Chapter 12: Case Example—EFFT and Traumatic Loss
The clinical strategy for thawing frozen grief: build emotional safety, invite one small piece of the trauma narrative at a time, and immediately orchestrate a caregiving response before going further. Includes the red flag of handing a crying client a tissue and letting them weep alone, plus the enactment prompts that bring the family in.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 12: Case Example—EFFT and Traumatic Loss
The clinical strategy for thawing frozen grief: build emotional safety, invite one small piece of the trauma narrative at a time, and immediately orchestrate a caregiving response before going further. Includes the red flag of handing a crying client a tissue and letting them weep alone, plus the enactment prompts that bring the family in.

item.title: Ep 53 · Ch 12 — Layla's Dissociation and the Anchoring Power of Touch
item.description:
Chapter 12: Case Example—EFFT and Traumatic Loss
A close read of the case of a sixteen-year-old who found her brother after his suicide and dissociates whenever the day is raised, while her guilt-flooded parents look away. Listeners learn why steering off the topic to "protect" her confirms the trauma is unspeakable, and how grounding plus parental physical contact lets her process a fraction of the memory safely.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 12: Case Example—EFFT and Traumatic Loss
A close read of the case of a sixteen-year-old who found her brother after his suicide and dissociates whenever the day is raised, while her guilt-flooded parents look away. Listeners learn why steering off the topic to "protect" her confirms the trauma is unspeakable, and how grounding plus parental physical contact lets her process a fraction of the memory safely.

item.title: Ep 54 · Ch 12 — Blocking the Silver-Lining Bypass in Traumatic Grief
item.description:
Chapter 12: Case Example—EFFT and Traumatic Loss
Why premature meaning-making and "at least..." statements shut grief down, and how to gently block the bypass when a parent reaches for the good times while their child is still in agony. Includes the self-of-the-therapist question of whether your own anxiety is what makes closure feel urgent.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 12: Case Example—EFFT and Traumatic Loss
Why premature meaning-making and "at least..." statements shut grief down, and how to gently block the bypass when a parent reaches for the good times while their child is still in agony. Includes the self-of-the-therapist question of whether your own anxiety is what makes closure feel urgent.

item.title: Ep 55 · Ch 12 — Vicarious Trauma: Bearing Witness Without Numbing Out
item.description:
Chapter 12: Case Example—EFFT and Traumatic Loss
What happens to the clinician's own attachment system when sitting with a family whose child died by suicide, from the crushing chest weight to intrusive thoughts and the urge to avoid the family. Listeners learn to spot the twin traps of compartmentalizing and enmeshment, and to redefine success as the family feeling their pain together.
item.itunes:author: EFFT Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 12: Case Example—EFFT and Traumatic Loss
What happens to the clinician's own attachment system when sitting with a family whose child died by suicide, from the crushing chest weight to intrusive thoughts and the urge to avoid the family. Listeners learn to spot the twin traps of compartmentalizing and enmeshment, and to redefine success as the family feeling their pain together.
